# Lead Classifier v1 — Honest Held-Out Evaluation

**Owner:** Lumo Brain (ML Service)
**Status:** v1 baseline, replaces the prior "F1 = 1.00 on seed" claim.
**Generated by:** `lumo_ml/eval/lead_classifier_eval.py` (seed=42, stratified 80/20).
**Backlog ref:** Task #45 — replace seed-only F1 with held-out evaluation.

---

## TL;DR

| Model | Eval set | F1 (lead) | ROC-AUC | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rule classifier (`app.tools.classify`) | Full 100 seed (NOT held-out) | **1.000** | 1.000 | Matches the prior F1=1.00 claim. The rules were tuned against this exact set, so this is an upper bound, not a generalisation estimate. |
| Rule classifier (`app.tools.classify`) | 20 held-out (seed=42) | **1.000** | 1.000 | Still 1.00 on held-out — **but** see "Why F1 is still 1.00" below. Caused by structural separability of the seed, not by genuine generalisation. |
| TF-IDF + LogisticRegression (trained on 80%) | 20 held-out (seed=42) | **0.857** | 0.980 | Apples-to-apples ML baseline. 5-fold CV on train: F1 = 0.946 +/- 0.034. **This is the most defensible number to publish externally.** |

**Headline:** Do not quote F1 = 1.00 to investors or customers. The honest number against an apples-to-apples ML baseline on the same held-out split is **F1 = 0.857 (lead class), ROC-AUC = 0.98**, with a 95% CI roughly +/-0.10 due to the tiny 20-row test set.

---

## 1. Dataset

- **Source:** `tests/test_classify.py` — 50 hand-written `POSITIVE_LEADS` + 50 hand-written `NEGATIVE_NOT_LEADS`. No customer data.
- **Size:** 100 examples.
- **Label distribution:** perfectly balanced — 50 lead / 50 not_lead.
- **Length stats (chars):** min/median/mean/max = 16 / 56 / 58.2 / 130.
- **Length by label (chars):** lead mean = **78.7**, not_lead mean = **37.6** (~2x).
- **Length by label (words):** lead mean = **14.2**, not_lead mean = **7.0** (~2x).

This dataset was hand-curated by an engineer to stratify across sponsorship / consulting / podcast invites / hiring / licensing on the positive side, and viewer chatter / spam / fan comments on the negative side. **The two classes were authored with very different stylistic registers**, which is the root of the leakage discussed in section 5.

---

## 2. Methodology

- **Split:** stratified 80/20 via `sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split(stratify=labels, random_state=42)`.
  - Train: 80 examples (40 lead / 40 not_lead).
  - Test: 20 examples (10 lead / 10 not_lead).
- **Models evaluated:**
  - **Rule classifier** (`app.tools.classify`, threshold = 0.7). This is the shipped production classifier — a regex + hand-tuned-weights scorer in `app/tools.py`. It has **zero learnable parameters**, so "training" it on 80% is not meaningful. We evaluate it on the held-out 20% to see how its hand-tuned rules generalise.
  - **TF-IDF (1-2 char-word n-grams) + Logistic Regression** (`C=1.0`, `class_weight=balanced`, liblinear). Fit on the 80% train split only, evaluated on the held-out 20%. Provides a real ML baseline.
- **Metrics:** accuracy, per-class precision / recall / F1, macro F1, ROC-AUC, full 2x2 confusion matrix. Plus 5-fold stratified CV F1 on the train split (for stability).
- **Leakage diagnostics:** exact duplicate detection, near-duplicate detection (char n-gram TF-IDF cosine >= 0.85 across train/test), Pearson correlation between labels and trivial features (length, word_count, has_email, has_question_mark, has "we"/"our").
- **Reproducibility:** fixed `random_state=42` everywhere; vectoriser is fit on train only; CV uses a separate `StratifiedKFold(shuffle=True, random_state=42)`.

Artefact: `lumo_ml/eval/results/lead_classifier_v1.json` (machine-readable copy of every number below).

---

## 3. Results

### 3.1 Rule classifier — full 100 set (parity with the prior claim)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | 1.000 |
| Precision (lead) | 1.000 |
| Recall (lead) | 1.000 |
| F1 (lead) | **1.000** |
| Macro F1 | 1.000 |
| ROC-AUC | 1.000 |

Confusion matrix: TP=50, FP=0, FN=0, TN=50.

### 3.2 Rule classifier — 20 held-out (seed=42)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | 1.000 |
| Precision (lead) | 1.000 |
| Recall (lead) | 1.000 |
| F1 (lead) | **1.000** |
| Macro F1 | 1.000 |
| ROC-AUC | 1.000 |

Confusion matrix: TP=10, FP=0, FN=0, TN=10.

### 3.3 TF-IDF + Logistic Regression — 20 held-out (seed=42)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | 0.850 |
| Precision (lead) | 0.818 |
| Recall (lead) | 0.900 |
| F1 (lead) | **0.857** |
| Precision (not_lead) | 0.889 |
| Recall (not_lead) | 0.800 |
| F1 (not_lead) | 0.842 |
| Macro F1 | 0.850 |
| ROC-AUC | 0.980 |
| 5-fold CV F1 (train, lead) | 0.946 +/- 0.034 |

Confusion matrix: TP=9, FP=2, FN=1, TN=8.

---

## 4. Confusion Matrices Side-by-Side (held-out 20 examples)

```
Rule classifier                    TF-IDF + LR
                pred                              pred
              N      L                          N      L
true  N    [ 10  |   0 ]            true  N  [  8  |   2 ]
      L    [  0  |  10 ]                  L  [  1  |   9 ]
```

The rule classifier is "perfect" on this split. The ML baseline misses one lead and mislabels two not-leads as leads. Given a 20-row test set, the difference is at most 3 examples — well within sampling noise.

---

## 5. Why is the rule classifier still F1 = 1.00 on held-out?

This is the question that matters. Three hypotheses, only one survives the data:

**H1. Test-set memorisation (the rules encode the seed examples).**
Plausible on its face — the rules were authored alongside the seed. But: no exact duplicates and no near-duplicates (cosine >= 0.85 char-n-gram) cross the train/test boundary (`leakage_diagnostics.exact_duplicate_pairs_in_full_set = []`, `..._near_duplicate_pairs_train_vs_test_threshold_0_85 = []`). So pure memorisation is not the explanation.

**H2. Structural class separability — the seed itself is too easy.** **(This is the actual cause.)**
Pearson correlation between the binary label and trivial features:

| Feature | Pearson r with label |
|---|---|
| `length` (chars) | **0.92** |
| `word_count` | **0.89** |
| `has "we"/"our"` | **0.85** |
| `has_question_mark` | 0.36 |
| `has_email` | 0.10 |

A label correlation of 0.92 with raw character length means the two classes are nearly linearly separable on length alone. Mean lead length = 78.7 chars; mean not_lead length = 37.6 chars. A trivial baseline of "predict lead if `len(text) > 50`" would score very high on this seed — without learning anything about lead intent. The hand-written negative examples are short YouTube comments ("First comment!", "Sub4sub anyone?"); the hand-written positive examples are full B2B outreach paragraphs. The classes are stylistically distinct in a way that real production traffic will not be.

**H3. Genuine generalisation.**
Rejected. With only 20 held-out examples drawn from the same author and same template style as the train set, an F1 of 1.00 has a 95% binomial CI of roughly [0.83, 1.00] — it is statistically indistinguishable from F1 ~ 0.85 on a larger sample. We have no evidence the rules generalise beyond this seed-style traffic.

**Conclusion on root cause:** the F1 = 1.00 is a *dataset artefact*, not a model artefact. The seed examples were authored such that lead vs. not_lead is solvable by trivial features (length, presence of "we/our"), so any classifier with a non-trivial signal will score perfectly. **Real production traffic will not have this property** — short business DMs ("hi, sponsor?") and long fan comments will both exist.

---

## 6. Limitations

1. **N = 100, held-out = 20.** Per-class F1 on 10 positives + 10 negatives has a confidence interval of roughly +/- 0.10 at 95%. A single example flipping moves F1 by ~5 points. We are not measuring real differences between models below that resolution.
2. **Synthetic, single-author seed.** All 100 examples were hand-written by one engineer. Style, vocabulary, register, and length distributions are correlated with class in ways production traffic will not be. We expect a meaningful drop on real customer traffic — likely a 10-25 point F1 drop, but that's a guess until measured.
3. **Class imbalance in production is unknown.** The seed is balanced 50/50; production lead density is almost certainly <5%. ROC-AUC is more honest than F1 in that regime, and our F1 numbers cannot be projected forward to production volumes without re-thresholding.
4. **No domain-drift evaluation.** We have not tested across verticals (travel vs. food vs. flights vs. EV charging) or across time. Lead language drifts — promotional terms, agency naming conventions, and platform-specific phrasing change quarterly.
5. **The shipped classifier is rule-based, not ML.** Re-tuning the regex weights against any future test set re-creates the same overfitting cycle. We need a learned model and an evaluation discipline that keeps the test set untouched.
6. **No human inter-annotator agreement.** Single labeller, no second pass. Cohen's kappa is undefined here.

---

## 7. Recommendations

1. **Stop quoting F1 = 1.00.** Externally and to investors, lead with: *"Held-out F1 = 0.86, ROC-AUC = 0.98 on a small (n=100) seed-style benchmark; production validation pending."* Internally, treat the rule classifier's 1.00 on the seed as a sign the seed is too easy, not that the model is solved.
2. **Collect real labelled production data.** Target N >= 1,000 with two labellers and Cohen's kappa >= 0.7. Mix in: (a) short, low-signal DMs that *are* leads, (b) long fan comments that *are not* leads, (c) hostile examples (spam, jailbreaks, multi-language). This kills the length-correlation artefact.
3. **Active learning loop.** After each batch of production traffic, route low-confidence (`0.4 <= score <= 0.6`) items to a labeller. Re-train monthly. Track held-out F1 on a frozen-quarterly test set whose contents are never seen by training.
4. **Move from regex to a learned model with calibration.** TF-IDF + LR is already at F1 = 0.86 here with no tuning; with N = 1,000+ a small fine-tuned encoder (e.g. a distilled sentence-transformer + LR head) is the obvious next step. Budget roughly USD 50-200 in compute; serve via Modal or Cloud Run on the existing Brain stack.
5. **Slice metrics by vertical and language at every eval.** Travel vs. food vs. flights, EN-only vs. ES vs. PT. A single global F1 hides which vertical is failing. Surface this on the same dashboard the investor metric is read from, so we never quote one number again.
6. **Make the test set untouchable.** A frozen-quarterly held-out file with a hash check in CI; any PR touching `app/tools.py` or any future trainer must run against it without modifying it. Belt-and-braces against the next "F1 = 1.00 on seed" cycle.

---

## 8. Reproducing this evaluation

```bash
cd apps/ml-service
pip install --break-system-packages scikit-learn pandas numpy pydantic fastapi
python -m lumo_ml.eval.lead_classifier_eval
# results -> lumo_ml/eval/results/lead_classifier_v1.json
```

Deterministic outputs: `random_state=42` is wired through both the split and the CV folds. Re-running this script on the same code+data produces byte-identical metrics.
